original_abstract: Invasive fungal infections (IFIs) have emerged as a significant global health threat, characterized by high morbidity and mortality rates particularly among the growing population of immunocompromised patients. Early and precise identification of fungal pathogens is essential for initiating life-saving antifungal therapy; however, traditional diagnostic methods, such as culture and microscopy, are often hindered by low sensitivity, long turnaround times, and the challenges posed by emerging multidrug-resistant and cryptic species. This review provides a comprehensive analysis of the recent technological shifts in clinical mycology. We examine significant advancements in non-culture-based tools, including legal-approved molecular platforms like T2 Candida, digital droplet PCR, and metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS). Furthermore, the review explores the frontier of fungal diagnostics, highlighting the development of novel biosensors, microfluidic "lab-on-a-chip" technologies, and the integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning models for automated image and data analysis. By synthesizing these diverse innovations—from point-of-care lateral flow assays to sophisticated bioinformatic approaches—this paper updates the scientific community on the evolving diagnostic landscape and discusses how these modern adjuncts can be integrated into clinical algorithms to improve patient outcomes and antimicrobial stewardship.
humanized_abstract: Invasive fungal infections (IFIs) are considered emerging global health crisis with high morbidity and mortality, primarily affecting the ever growing pool of immunocompromised patients. Rapid and accurate detection of fungal pathogens is essential for the institution of effective treatment using targeted antifungal therapy. The conventional clinical methods for detection using culture and/or microscopy are hampered due to low sensitivity, longer turn-around-time, and emergence of drug-resistant fungi (including Cryptococci) and increasing incidence of so called 'cryptic' species. The present review attempts to capture the recent developments in the field of clinical mycology with a focus towards molecular diagnostics as well as novel approaches including the non-culture-based techniques using legally approved molecular tests, e.g., T2 Candida, digital droplet PCR (dd-PCR), and metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS). Moreover, it explores recent discoveries related to the development of advanced fungal detection using a range of biosensors, "lab-on-a-chip" microfluidics approaches, as well as applications of artificial intelligence and machine learning that could potentially analyse image and/or pattern rich data related to host-pathogen interactions. Collectively, combining point-of-care laterally flow tests, to sophisticated bioinformatic tools and techniques for high-throughput data analyses discussed in this review shall aid in realizing the developments in the modern adjuncts for clinical mycology diagnostics. These advancements along with their scope and expected impact in the development of effective clinical workflows or algorithms will ensure improved treatment strategies for IFIs while advancing the principles of antimicrobial stewardship in the modern health-care systems.
